Grassland Assessment
Report 2025

Canadian Prairie Ecozone  ·  Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba

Grassland Assessment Grassland Dynamics Mapping Grassland Distribution Accuracy Assessment
Prepared For
Canadian Forage & Grassland Association
Prepared By
Grassland Analytica Corp.
Report Date
June 2026
Study Years
2023 (baseline) & 2025
Section 01

Executive Summary

The 2025 Grassland Assessment provides a comprehensive evaluation of the current distribution, composition, and condition of grassland ecosystems across the Canadian Prairie ecozone. The assessment was undertaken to generate an up-to-date, high-resolution inventory of grassland resources and to quantify landscape changes that have occurred since the previous assessment conducted in 2023. The resulting information supports evidence-based decision-making for grassland conservation planning, sustainable land management, agricultural policy, ecological monitoring, and long-term environmental reporting.

The assessment was developed using multi-temporal Sentinel-2 Level-2A satellite imagery acquired throughout the 2025 growing season. Satellite observations were processed within the Google Earth Engine (GEE) cloud-computing environment using a two-stage supervised machine learning approach based on a Random Forest classification workflow specifically designed to improve discrimination among ecologically similar grassland communities. The methodology first classified major land-cover types before applying a dedicated grassland classification model to distinguish three ecologically important grassland classes: Native Grassland, Tame Grassland, and Mixed Grassland. The workflow integrated spectral reflectance, vegetation indices, seasonal phenological metrics, texture measures, and topographic information to maximize classification accuracy and spatial consistency.

The 2025 assessment identified approximately 14.52 million hectares of classified grassland across the study region, representing a modest increase of approximately 166,600 hectares (1.2%) relative to the 2023 baseline. While the overall grassland extent remained relatively stable, the analysis revealed measurable shifts in grassland composition. Mixed Grassland exhibited the largest increase, expanding by approximately 480,200 hectares (24.9%), whereas Tame Grassland declined by approximately 235,600 hectares (5.8%). Native Grassland remained the dominant grassland type, experiencing only a slight reduction in total mapped area while continuing to represent the majority of the region's grassland resource.

A comprehensive pixel-by-pixel change detection analysis revealed that 69.3% of all grassland pixels retained the same classification over the two-year period, demonstrating a high degree of landscape stability. The remaining changes primarily reflected transitions among the three grassland classes, attributable to localized vegetation dynamics, management practices, ecological succession, and seasonal variability rather than widespread landscape transformation.

The first-stage land-cover classification achieved an Overall Accuracy exceeding 99%, providing a highly reliable foundation for subsequent grassland mapping. The second-stage grassland classification achieved an Overall Accuracy of approximately 71%. Native Grassland consistently exhibited the highest classification accuracy, while Mixed Grassland remained the most challenging class due to its transitional ecological characteristics.

Client Interpretation. The strongest management signal is the expansion of Mixed Grassland and the decline of Tame Grassland. Native Grassland remains the dominant mapped class. Mixed Grassland changes should be interpreted alongside local management records, weather conditions, and field validation where available.
Section 02

Project Overview

Canada's Prairie grasslands represent one of the country's most important ecological and agricultural landscapes, supporting livestock production, biodiversity conservation, carbon sequestration, water regulation, and numerous ecosystem services. Effective stewardship of these landscapes requires reliable, consistent, and up-to-date information on grassland distribution and composition to support evidence-based decision-making across local, provincial, and national scales.

To address this need, the Canadian Forage and Grassland Association (CFGA) commissioned this project to produce a comprehensive satellite-based assessment of grassland resources across the Canadian Prairie ecozone for the 2025 growing season. The assessment provides a standardized inventory of the spatial distribution of Native, Tame, and Mixed Grasslands, while also documenting changes relative to the previous regional assessment completed in 2023. The resulting products support conservation planning, sustainable grazing management, policy development, environmental reporting, and future national grassland monitoring initiatives.

This project builds upon a series of grassland mapping and monitoring studies developed by the authors over recent years. Previous work established operational machine learning frameworks for mapping prairie grasslands using multispectral satellite imagery and developed regional grassland classification products for Manitoba and the Canadian Prairies (Badreldin et al., 2023; Badreldin et al., 2024; Mousavi et al., 2025). The experience gained through these studies informed the design of the current workflow, including feature engineering, hierarchical classification, accuracy assessment, and change detection methodologies.

Study Area

The study covers the Canadian Prairie ecozone, including the agricultural and grassland regions of Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba, extending approximately from 116°W to 96°W longitude and 49°N to 54°N latitude. The study area was divided into 12 processing tiles mosaicked to generate seamless regional products. All datasets were processed and delivered in the NAD83 / Statistics Canada Lambert projection (EPSG:3347) — the standard coordinate reference system for national-scale environmental mapping in Canada.

Input Data & Processing Parameters

Table 1. Summary of input data sources and processing parameters used in the 2025 Grassland Assessment.

ParameterValue / Description
Primary satellite dataSentinel-2 Level-2A Surface Reflectance (Copernicus Programme)
Assessment year2025 — 2023 products used as the baseline for change detection
Study areaCanadian Prairie ecozone — Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba
Geographic extentApproximately 116°W–96°W longitude and 49°N–54°N latitude
Processing platformGoogle Earth Engine cloud computing platform
Coordinate reference systemNAD83 / Statistics Canada Lambert (EPSG:3347)
Spatial resolution10 m × 10 m — 100 m² per pixel — 0.01 ha
Atmospheric correctionSentinel-2 Level-2A surface reflectance — Sen2Cor processing chain
Cloud maskingCloud Score+ quality assessment dataset — threshold ≤ 0.80
Seasonal compositesMedian composites for May–June, July–August, and September–October
Seasonal rationaleEarly growth, peak biomass, and late-season senescence to capture phenological variability
Radiometric scalingSurface reflectance values scaled by 1/10,000
Band harmonizationAll spectral bands resampled to a common 10 m spatial resolution
Topographic datasetShuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM) Digital Elevation Model (DEM)
Derived terrain variablesElevation, slope, and aspect
Output tiling12 GeoTIFF tiles (LZW compressed) mosaicked into seamless regional products

Sentinel-2 Spectral Bands

Table 2. Sentinel-2 spectral bands selected for the 2025 Grassland Assessment and their primary applications in this study.

BandCentral Wavelength (nm)Native ResolutionPrimary Application
B2 – Blue49010 mWater detection, bare soil and built-up area discrimination
B3 – Green56010 mVegetation vigour, plant reflectance, vegetation community differentiation
B4 – Red66510 mChlorophyll absorption; used in NDVI and other vegetation indices
B6 – Red Edge 274020 m (resampled to 10 m)Sensitive to chlorophyll concentration and vegetation stress; improves grassland subtype discrimination
B8 – Near Infrared (NIR)84210 mBiomass estimation, canopy structure, vegetation vigour, and texture analysis
B12 – Shortwave Infrared (SWIR)219020 m (resampled to 10 m)Vegetation moisture, senescence, soil moisture, and separation of grassland communities

Section 03

Methodology

The 2025 Grassland Assessment was developed using a standardized multi-stage remote sensing workflow. Multi-temporal Sentinel-2 imagery acquired throughout the growing season was preprocessed and transformed into spectral, phenological, texture, and topographic predictor variables. A hierarchical Random Forest classification framework was then applied to first classify major land-cover classes and subsequently distinguish Native, Tame, and Mixed Grasslands.

Classification Pipeline

1
Data Collection

Sentinel-2 L2A imagery; Cloud Score+ masking (≤ 0.80); 3 seasonal median composites

2
Feature Engineering

54 candidate features: spectral bands, vegetation indices, GLCM textures, phenological metrics, terrain variables

3
Stage 1 — Land Cover

Random Forest (150 trees); 5 classes: Cropland, Grassland, Water, Woodland, Man-Made

4
Grassland Mask

Pixels classified as Grassland in Stage 1 are isolated for subtype classification

5
Stage 2 — Grassland Types

Random Forest on top 20 features; 3 classes: Tame, Native, Mixed

Spectral Feature Engineering

To maximize the separability of grassland communities, multiple feature types were derived from the Sentinel-2 imagery. The workflow integrates spectral, temporal, spatial, and environmental information to capture the complexity of prairie ecosystems. This multi-source feature space substantially improves discrimination among ecologically similar grassland classes.

Table 3. Summary of feature groups used in the classification workflow.

Feature GroupVariablesPurpose
Spectral BandsB2, B3, B4, B6, B8, B12 (all three seasons)Capture spectral signatures of vegetation, soil, and water
Vegetation IndicesNDVI, SAVI, EVI, RVI, RDVI, NDVI Red-Edge, NDBIDescribe vegetation vigour, biomass, chlorophyll, and moisture content
Phenological MetricsNDVI growth, decline, peak, minimum, amplitude; NIR changeCharacterize seasonal vegetation dynamics and growing-season patterns
GLCM Texture (NIR)Contrast, Entropy, Variance, Sum AverageCapture spatial heterogeneity and vegetation canopy structure
Terrain VariablesElevation, slope, aspect (SRTM DEM)Represent environmental controls on vegetation distribution
Feature selection: Random Forest feature importance analysis was applied to reduce 54 candidate features to the 20 most influential variables, improving model efficiency and reducing the risk of overfitting.

Training Sample Design

To improve the robustness and generalizability of the classification models, training samples were subjected to a spatial buffering constraint. A minimum separation distance of 10 m and a maximum proximity threshold of 11 m were enforced between neighbouring training samples, ensuring no two samples represented the same or immediately adjacent pixels. This spatial filtering reduces spatial autocorrelation — a common source of overly optimistic classification accuracy — while preserving the spatial representativeness of local vegetation conditions.

ClassTraining SamplesProportion
Native Grassland4,79345.8%
Tame Grassland3,50733.5%
Mixed Grassland2,18220.8%
Total10,482100%

Change Detection Methodology

Grassland dynamics between 2023 and 2025 were quantified through a post-classification comparison, in which the independently classified grassland maps for each assessment year were compared on a pixel-by-pixel basis. Prior to comparison, both mosaics were aligned to the same spatial reference system (EPSG:3347), spatial resolution (10 m), and pixel grid.

Each pixel was assigned a unique transition code: Transition Code = Class2023 × 10 + Class2025. Transition areas were calculated as: Area (ha) = Pixel Count × 0.01 ha.

CodeInterpretationType
22Tame Grassland → Tame GrasslandStable
33Native Grassland → Native GrasslandStable
55Mixed Grassland → Mixed GrasslandStable
23Tame Grassland → Native GrasslandTransition
25Tame Grassland → Mixed GrasslandTransition
32Native Grassland → Tame GrasslandTransition
35Native Grassland → Mixed GrasslandTransition
52Mixed Grassland → Tame GrasslandTransition
53Mixed Grassland → Native GrasslandTransition
Section 04

2025 Land Cover Assessment

Overview

The first stage of the classification workflow produced a comprehensive 2025 land-cover map for the Canadian Prairie study area, providing the spatial foundation for the subsequent grassland assessment. Using multi-temporal Sentinel-2 imagery and a supervised Random Forest classifier, the landscape was categorized into five major land-cover classes: Cropland, Grassland, Woodland, Water, and Man-Made areas.

The resulting land-cover map demonstrates the dominant agricultural character of the Canadian Prairies, where cropland and grassland together occupy the majority of the landscape. Woodland is primarily associated with riparian corridors, shelterbelts, and forested regions, while water bodies and developed areas represent comparatively small proportions of the mapped area — reflecting the characteristic mosaic of agricultural production and natural ecosystems across Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba.

Land Cover Distribution 2025 (estimated proportions)

Stage 1 Classification Accuracy by Class — 2025

Classification Performance

The Stage 1 land-cover classification achieved an Overall Accuracy of 99.20% with a Kappa coefficient of 0.987, indicating excellent agreement between the classified map and the independent validation dataset. Producer's Accuracy and User's Accuracy exceeded 98% for all land-cover classes.

Land Cover ClassProducer's Accuracy (PA)User's Accuracy (UA)
Cropland99.52%99.17%
Grassland98.93%99.24%
Man-Made98.23%96.94%
Water98.86%99.90%
Woodland98.53%98.77%
Overall Accuracy99.20%  |  Kappa: 0.987
Exceptional accuracy. Stage 1 achieved 99.20% Overall Accuracy, confirming that the grassland mask used to drive Stage 2 is highly reliable. Virtually all pixels entering the grassland sub-classification were genuinely grassland.
Section 05 — Primary Deliverable

2025 Grassland Assessment

The 2025 grassland classification identifies and quantifies three ecologically distinct grassland types across the Canadian Prairie ecozone. This section presents the spatial distribution, area statistics, and ecological interpretation for each class.

Interactive Grassland Classification Map — 2025

The interactive map below provides a seamless visualization of the 2025 grassland classification across the Canadian Prairie ecozone. Users may zoom, pan, and navigate throughout the study area to examine localized grassland patterns. The classified grassland layer is displayed at 82% opacity over ESRI World Imagery with place-name labels. Toggle the layer control to show or hide the grassland overlay.

Ecological Interpretation

Native Grassland

Native Grassland is the dominant mapped class, comprising 56.9% of the classified area in 2025 (8,262,857 ha). It showed a modest net decline of 78,002 ha (−0.9%) compared to 2023. Native grasslands are characterized by diverse species assemblages of perennial grasses and forbs that have not been subjected to cultivation. Their spectral distinctiveness — particularly in red-edge NDVI and late-season SWIR — makes them the most accurately classified grassland type (Producer's Accuracy: 84.76%).

Tame Grassland

Tame Grassland declined by 235,565 ha (−5.8%), the largest absolute decrease among the three classes. Tame grasslands are established, managed pastures — typically seeded with introduced species such as smooth brome, crested wheatgrass, or alfalfa. This decline may reflect genuine conversion to cropland, or reclassification of borderline tame areas as Mixed due to changes in phenological signal. Field validation is recommended for areas showing this transition.

Mixed Grassland

Mixed Grassland showed the largest proportional increase: +480,178 ha (+24.9%). Mixed Grassland represents transitional zones where native and tame characteristics co-occur, or where vegetation communities are in flux. The increase is likely driven by a combination of genuine vegetation dynamics and improved classifier performance from auxiliary training samples in 2025. This class carries the highest classification uncertainty (PA: 52.88%) and should be prioritized for field review.

Section 06

Classification Accuracy

Accuracy was evaluated using a hold-out validation set (20% of samples) and standard confusion-matrix metrics: Overall Accuracy (OA), Producer's Accuracy (PA), User's Accuracy (UA), and the Kappa coefficient (κ). PA indicates omission error (from the reference-data perspective); UA indicates commission error (from the user's perspective).

Overall Accuracy — Stage 1: Land Cover

202399.18%  κ = 0.986
202599.20%  κ = 0.987

Overall Accuracy — Stage 2: Grassland Types

202371.92%  κ = 0.561
202570.71%  κ = 0.540

2025 Grassland Class Accuracy — PA & UA

2025 Grassland Type Accuracy

ClassProducer's Accuracy (PA)User's Accuracy (UA)Interpretation
Tame64.78%72.64%Moderate — some omission from confusion with Mixed
Native84.76%74.63%Good — spectrally distinct; highest PA of the three classes
Mixed52.88%59.38%Lower — transitional class; highest uncertainty
OverallOA: 70.71%  |  Kappa: 0.540Comparable to 2023; consistent workflow performance
Reliability note. Grassland sub-classification is inherently more challenging than broad land-cover mapping because Tame, Native, and Mixed grasslands share similar spectral and phenological signatures — especially in transition zones. The Mixed class should be treated as the highest-uncertainty class and prioritized for field-based review. Results are most reliable for Native Grassland and least reliable for Mixed Grassland.
Section 07

2023–2025 Change Detection

Why 2023 as the baseline? The 2023 classification used an identical methodology — same features, same classifier, same seasonal windows — making it a directly comparable baseline. Both years achieved similar Overall Accuracies, confirming that observed transitions reflect genuine landscape dynamics rather than workflow artefacts. Both mosaics were aligned to the same spatial reference, resolution, and pixel grid prior to comparison.

Grassland Transition Flows — Sankey Diagram

The diagram below shows how grassland area (in hectares) transitioned between classes from 2023 (left) to 2025 (right). Link width is proportional to area. Hover over any flow to see the transition details, area, and percentage of the total grassland.

Stable vs. Changed Area

Stable vs. Changed Grassland Area

8,693,055 ha (69.3%) remained in the same grassland class across both years — demonstrating a high degree of landscape stability across the Prairie ecozone.
3,856,206 ha (30.7%) transitioned to a different grassland class, reflecting localized vegetation dynamics, management practices, ecological succession, and seasonal variability.
Section 08

Key Findings

01

Native Grassland Remains Dominant

At 8.26 million ha, Native Grassland accounts for 56.9% of all classified grassland in 2025. Despite a modest net decline of 78,000 ha (−0.9%), it retains its position as the most extensive and most accurately mapped class across the Prairie ecozone, reflecting the overall persistence of native prairie ecosystems.

02

Mixed Grassland Expanded Significantly

Mixed Grassland grew by approximately 480,000 ha (+24.9%), the largest proportional change. This warrants further investigation — it may reflect genuine vegetation succession, management change, or the influence of improved training samples in 2025. Repeated annual assessments will improve confidence in identifying persistent trends.

03

Tame Grassland Contracted

Tame Grassland declined by approximately 236,000 ha (−5.8%), the largest absolute decrease. This could signal land use conversion to cropland, abandonment of managed pastures, or reclassification as Mixed due to phenological signal changes. Areas transitioning from Tame to Mixed are highest-priority for field validation.

04

Overall Grassland Extent Stable

The total classified grassland area increased by approximately 167,000 ha (+1.2%), indicating that the Prairie grassland resource base remains broadly intact between assessment years, providing a reliable foundation for long-term monitoring and conservation planning.

05

69% Landscape Stability

Of all grassland pixels classified in both years, 69.3% (8.69M ha) retained the same grassland type — indicating broad landscape stability. This high degree of persistence provides a reliable baseline for tracking future change and evaluating the effectiveness of conservation initiatives.

06

Consistent Workflow Performance

Stage 2 accuracy was 70.71% OA (Kappa 0.540) in 2025, closely matching 71.92% OA in 2023. This consistency confirms that observed landscape changes are real signals rather than artefacts of methodology drift, validating the framework for annual repeat assessments.

Section 09

Management Implications

The 2025 grassland assessment delivers a practical decision-support tool for identifying areas that may require management attention, ecological assessment, or continued monitoring. The observed transitions between 2023 and 2025 should be interpreted as indicators of potential landscape change rather than definitive confirmation of ecological processes. Change detection results are best used to prioritize locations for field verification, targeted monitoring, and integration with complementary environmental datasets.

Areas Requiring Priority Monitoring

Pixels transitioning from Tame → Mixed (705,883 ha) and from Native → Mixed (448,448 ha) represent the highest-priority areas for monitoring and field validation. These transitions may reflect:

  • Changes in grazing intensity or management practices
  • Natural succession and vegetation recovery processes
  • Encroachment of non-characteristic plant species
  • Interannual differences in vegetation phenology or climatic conditions
  • Classification uncertainty associated with spectrally similar grassland communities

Regions Experiencing Significant Landscape Dynamics

The pixel-level change raster (change_map_2023_2025.tif) can be integrated within GIS alongside additional spatial datasets to identify specific management units experiencing the most change:

  • Agricultural land parcels, grazing leases, and allotments
  • Provincial Crown lands, conservation easements, and protected areas
  • Indigenous Protected and Conserved Areas (IPCAs)
  • Watershed boundaries and municipal planning layers
  • Ecological site classifications and grassland conservation priorities

Restoration Opportunities

The Tame → Native transition (914,775 ha) may indicate areas where seeded grasslands are developing vegetation communities with characteristics more similar to native prairie. If confirmed through ground surveys, these locations could represent important opportunities for:

  • Native prairie restoration initiatives and habitat enhancement programs
  • Long-term ecological monitoring and grassland stewardship agreements
  • Carbon sequestration projects and conservation incentive programs
  • Biodiversity conservation planning and Species-at-Risk habitat support

Carbon Storage & Biodiversity Considerations

Prairie grasslands play a fundamental role in supporting ecosystem services throughout western Canada. Native grasslands are widely recognized for their capacity to store substantial quantities of soil organic carbon, regulate hydrological processes, reduce soil erosion, and provide habitat for numerous Species at Risk.

The observed expansion of Mixed Grassland presents two possible interpretations requiring field evaluation:

  • Positive scenario: Mixed Grassland expansion reflects gradual recovery of previously managed grasslands toward more natural vegetation communities, enhancing habitat quality and carbon storage potential.
  • Alternative scenario: Mixed Grassland expansion reflects fragmentation or degradation of native prairie, resulting in reduced ecological integrity and increased vegetation heterogeneity.

Future Monitoring Recommendations

RecommendationPriorityRationale
Annual or biennial repeat classification (2027)HighDetect continued trends; confirm Mixed Grassland dynamics
Field validation of Tame → Mixed transition areasHighGround-truth the largest source of classification uncertainty
Integration with land parcel and tenure dataMediumIdentify specific management units driving change
Addition of 2021 or 2019 historical baselineMediumExtend time series; distinguish cyclical from directional trends
Integration with provincial soil carbon inventoriesMediumSupport carbon accounting and greenhouse gas reporting
Inclusion of LiDAR or SAR auxiliary dataLow–MediumImprove Mixed class accuracy and vegetation structure detection

B. Class Definitions

ClassPixel ValueDefinition
Tame Grassland2Established, managed pastures seeded with introduced grass or legume species (e.g., smooth brome, crested wheatgrass, alfalfa). Typically more uniform in spectral response and phenology.
Native Grassland3Unbroken, never-cultivated grassland dominated by native perennial grasses and forbs. Spectrally diverse; highest ecological and soil carbon value. Most accurately classified class.
Mixed Grassland5Transitional zones where native and tame characteristics co-occur, or where vegetation communities are in flux due to reversion, disturbance, or management change. Highest classification uncertainty.

Full definitions for each grassland class, including detailed ecological descriptions and classification criteria, can be found in Table 1 of: Mousavi, M.; Biney, J.K.M.; Kishchuk, B.; Youssef, A.; Cordeiro, M.R.C.; Friesen, G.; Cattani, D.; Namous, M.; Badreldin, N. A Hierarchical Machine Learning-Based Strategy for Mapping Grassland in Manitoba's Diverse Ecoregions. Remote Sens. 2024, 16, 4730. https://doi.org/10.3390/rs16244730

ParameterValue
ClassifierRandom Forest — 150 decision trees
Training/validation split80% training / 20% validation
Spatial resolution10 m × 10 m — 1 pixel = 0.01 ha
